editorialReading: TTF natgas is the European fertilizer story. When TTF rises, BASF, Yara and OCI idle European ammonia plants because importing finished product becomes cheaper than producing it — at which point Europe inhales more Gulf urea and ammonia at exactly the moment those flows are under threat. The 2022 gas crisis showed this dynamic at speed; the Iran war is bringing it back, but with a tighter physical supply ceiling on the other end. That ceiling is now showing: Egypt FOB urea has roughly doubled to around $800–850/t and TTF has eased to around €41–44/MWh, so the idle-and-import reflex is firing into a market with far less spare Gulf supply to sell.
watchNext: The May World Bank Pink Sheet has now landed, confirming the Hormuz-driven surge — the fertilizer index rose about 14% in April and urea hit a four-year high. Watch the June Pink Sheet, Argus weekly summaries and IFA Q2 commentary for whether the spike extends or rolls over, plus any further India / China export restrictions. A preliminary US-Iran ceasefire framework announced on 16 June has pushed TTF sharply lower and signals potential Hormuz reopening, but formal confirmation remains outstanding — if Gulf flows do not resume substantially before August, EU farmer prices for the autumn 2026 application window could remain elevated.
methodologyNote: Weekly editorial refresh from World Bank Pink Sheet (monthly, free), Argus public citations, Reuters / Bloomberg quotes, IFA quarterly summaries, and USDA fertilizer outlook. Gold-standard real-time prices (CRU, Argus, ICIS, Profercy) are paywalled — ranges are aggregated from publicly-cited figures, not republished feeds. Editorial reading is our market interpretation.
